metadata.title: FAPI 2.0 Security Profile - ISO/IEC 27002:2022 Mapping
metadata.remarks: This profile maps OpenID Foundation FAPI 2.0 Security Profile requirements to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 controls. It selects applicable controls and adds FAPI-specific assessment guidance. NOTE: An ISO 27002:2022 OSCAL catalog must be obtained separately due to ISO copyright restrictions.

props.name: keywords
props.value: FAPI, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, ISO 27001, ISO 27002, API Security, Financial-grade API

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Cryptographic Requirements: For JWT signatures, only PS256, ES256, or EdDSA (Ed25519) algorithms SHALL be used. The 'none' algorithm SHALL NOT be used or accepted. RSA keys SHALL have minimum 2048-bit length. Elliptic curve keys SHALL have minimum 224-bit length. All JWT processing SHALL adhere to RFC 8725 (JWT Best Practices).

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Retrieve JWKS from authorization server; verify all keys use PS256, ES256, or EdDSA
2. Verify RSA keys are at least 2048 bits
3. Verify EC keys use P-256 or stronger curves
4. Decode ID tokens; verify 'alg' header is PS256, ES256, or EdDSA
5. Submit JWT with alg=none; verify rejection
6. Submit JWT with HS256; verify rejection (symmetric not allowed for FAPI)

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Network Security Requirements: All endpoints SHALL only offer TLS-protected connections. TLS version 1.2 or later is required. TLS configuration SHALL follow BCP 195 recommendations. For browser-facing endpoints, HSTS with preload is required. Server certificate validation per RFC 9525 is mandatory.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Attempt HTTP connection to all endpoints; verify refused or redirect to HTTPS
2. Verify TLS 1.2+ is enabled; verify TLS 1.0/1.1 are disabled
3. Verify BCP 195 compliant cipher suites
4. Check HSTS headers on authorization endpoint
5. Present invalid certificates; verify rejection

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Authentication Information: Authorization servers SHALL only support confidential clients using MTLS (RFC 8705) or private_key_jwt (OIDC Core Section 9) for authentication. Client secrets (symmetric authentication) SHALL NOT be used. Credentials not intended for end-users SHALL have at least 128 bits of entropy.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Verify token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported contains only 'private_key_jwt', 'tls_client_auth', or 'self_signed_tls_client_auth'
2. Attempt token request with client_secret_basic; verify rejection
3. Attempt token request with client_secret_post; verify rejection
4. Test MTLS or private_key_jwt authentication; verify success

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Access Rights Management: Access tokens SHALL be sender-constrained via MTLS (RFC 8705) or DPoP (RFC 9449). PKCE with S256 is required for all authorization flows. Authorization codes SHALL be one-time use with maximum 60-second lifetime.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Obtain MTLS-bound token; verify cnf.x5t#S256 claim in introspection
2. Attempt to use token with different certificate; verify rejection
3. Obtain DPoP-bound token; verify cnf.jkt claim
4. Send PAR without code_challenge; verify rejection
5. Exchange authorization code; attempt reuse; verify rejection

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Information Access Restriction: Resource servers SHALL verify the validity, integrity, expiration, and revocation status of access tokens. Resource servers SHALL verify that the authorization represented by the access token is sufficient for the requested resource access. Access tokens SHALL only be accepted in the HTTP Authorization header.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Send request with valid access token; verify access granted
2. Send request with expired token; verify rejection
3. Send request with tampered JWT signature; verify rejection
4. Request resource without sufficient scope; verify 403
5. Send token in query parameter; verify rejection

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Secure Authentication: For private_key_jwt, the audience claim SHALL be the authorization server's issuer identifier. Authorization servers SHALL validate JWT signatures using keys from registered client JWKS. The 'iss' parameter (RFC 9207) SHALL be returned to prevent mix-up attacks.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Submit private_key_jwt with correct aud; verify acceptance
2. Submit private_key_jwt with wrong aud; verify rejection
3. Verify iss parameter in authorization response
4. Verify client rejects response with wrong iss

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Access Control: Sender-constrained access tokens provide cryptographic binding between the token and the client's key material. Resource servers SHALL verify sender-constraining (MTLS certificate thumbprint or DPoP proof) before granting access.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Obtain sender-constrained token (MTLS or DPoP)
2. Present token with correct key binding; verify access
3. Present token with different key; verify rejection
4. Verify RS checks cnf claim (x5t#S256 for MTLS, jkt for DPoP)

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Privileged Access Rights: Authorization servers SHALL only support confidential clients (no public clients). All OAuth flows use PKCE with S256. PAR (RFC 9126) is mandatory. Clients SHALL only send client_id and request_uri to the authorization endpoint.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Verify require_pushed_authorization_requests=true in metadata
2. Send authorization request without request_uri; verify rejection
3. Send PAR without client authentication; verify rejection
4. Verify all registered clients are confidential

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Web Service Security: Authorization servers SHALL NOT support CORS on the authorization endpoint. For TLS 1.2, only BCP 195 recommended cipher suites SHALL be used on server-to-server endpoints. JWKS endpoints SHALL be served over TLS.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Send OPTIONS with Origin header to authorization endpoint; verify no CORS headers
2. Enumerate cipher suites on token endpoint; verify BCP 195 recommended only
3. Verify JWKS endpoint served over HTTPS

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Secure Development Lifecycle - Input Validation: PAR (RFC 9126) is mandatory. The redirect_uri parameter SHALL be required in PAR requests. Authorization servers and clients SHALL NOT expose open redirectors. Clients SHALL verify the issuer claim in metadata matches the URL used to fetch it.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Send PAR without redirect_uri; verify rejection
2. Send PAR with unregistered redirect_uri; verify rejection
3. Test for open redirector vulnerabilities
4. Serve metadata with mismatched issuer; verify client rejects

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: FAPI 2.0 Application Security Requirements: Clients SHALL send access tokens only in HTTP headers (not query parameters). Clients SHALL protect authorization flow initiation against CSRF. Clients SHALL verify the iss parameter matches the expected authorization server.

parts.name: assessment-objects
parts.prose:
1. Capture client requests to RS; verify token in Authorization header only
2. Attempt CSRF attack on authorization initiation; verify protection
3. Modify iss in authorization response; verify client rejects

resources.title: ISO/IEC 27002:2022
resources.description: Information security, cybersecurity and privacy protection - Information security controls. NOTE: An OSCAL catalog for ISO 27002:2022 must be obtained or created separately due to ISO copyright restrictions.
resources.remarks: ISO 27002:2022 control IDs used in this profile: 5.15 (Access control), 5.17 (Authentication information), 5.18 (Access rights), 8.2 (Privileged access rights), 8.3 (Information access restriction), 8.5 (Secure authentication), 8.20 (Networks security), 8.21 (Security of network services), 8.24 (Use of cryptography), 8.25 (Secure development lifecycle), 8.26 (Application security requirements)
